answer:
In Mirza Ghalib's *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, the Urdu word **'umr'** ( عمر, literally "lifetime," "age," or "life span") frequently appears in the original Urdu text of his ghazals (each chapter is typically a ghazal comprising a series of rhyming couplets). It is rendered in English translations as "age," "lifetime," "life," or similar, and carries layered poetic significance tied to classical Urdu ghazal conventions: the **prolonged duration of love's suffering**, **the transience or impermanence of human existence**, **vows or devotion spanning one's entire life**, and **existential futility** in the face of unrequited longing or cosmic indifference.[^1][^2][^3]

### Key Themes and Examples
Ghalib uses *'umr'* hyperbolically to evoke the **interminable wait** of the lover, where emotional states (pain, sighs, patience) stretch across an epoch-like lifespan, contrasting with life's brevity.

- **Endless torment and patience in love**: The iconic opening couplet of ghazal 3 compares a lover's sigh (*aah*) to something requiring a full lifetime to produce any effect, emphasizing love's slow-burning agony.[^4]  
  > A sigh must wait **an age** to take its flight,[^5]  
  > Who lives to conquer your dark hair's long night?

- **Impermanence of vows and life**: In ghazal 27, a lover mocks a beloved's "lifelong" pledge of fidelity, noting that even *'umr'* itself lacks permanence—life decays, so why trust mortal promises? This reflects philosophical skepticism about human bonds.[^6]  
  > You swore a vow of faith **for life**, but pray,[^7]  
  > Does **life** itself not promise to decay?

- **Lifetime of endured hardship for love**: Ghazal 9 portrays a life (*umr bhar*) filled with troubles borne solely for the sake of the beloved's tangled locks, highlighting masochistic devotion amid ruin.[^8]  
  > **This lifetime** full of troubles that I've carried, filled with dread,[^9]  
  > Was all for you, O tangled curls, that beckon me ahead.

These motifs recur across Ghalib's oeuvre, where *'umr'* amplifies the ghazal's tension between eternal desire and mortal limits, often with ironic or paradoxical twists.[^10] The corpus does not exhaustively index every instance (239 chapters total), but *'umr'* consistently evokes life's exhaustive span as both curse and canvas for passion.[^11]

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 3 (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/03-aah-ko-chahiye-ik-umr-asar-hote-tak/translation/).
[^2]: Ibid., ch. 27 (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/27-dard-se-mere-hai-tujh-ko-be-qarari-haaye-haaye/translation/).
[^3]: Ibid., ch. 9 (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/09-ajab-nishaat-se-jallad-ke-chale-hain-hum-aage/translation/).
[^4]: Ibid., ch. 3 [p-0733e9](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/03-aah-ko-chahiye-ik-umr-asar-hote-tak/translation/#p-0733e9).
[^5]: Translates "ik umr."
[^6]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 27 [p-efe750](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/27-dard-se-mere-hai-tujh-ko-be-qarari-haaye-haaye/translation/#p-efe750).
[^7]: Translates "Umr bhar ka ... paimaan-e-wafa."
[^8]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 9 [p-49b2d8](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/09-ajab-nishaat-se-jallad-ke-chale-hain-hum-aage/translation/#p-49b2d8).
[^9]: Translates "Yeh umr bhar."
[^10]: See also ch. 13 and 28 for contextual echoes (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/13-baagh-pa-kar-khafqani-yeh-darata-hai-mujhe/translation/; /works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/28-deewangi-se-dosh-pe-zunnar-bhi-nahin/translation/).
[^11]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib* work wiki (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/wiki/_work.card.md).
